The Profit Center 	4_Write_Better_Copy_Faster 	Page 1 of 27
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 1 of 27
Bob Bly: 	So, welcome everyone to the Total Package Conference No. 3 in
our series. The title of today’s conference is Write Better Copy
Faster, produce up to twice as many projects per year, and get
bigger winners more often. So, this is everything you want.
You wanna get more packages out, you want more winners so
everybody wants you as a copywriter and you’re more productive
so you’re actually making more money on the advances, on the
front end in addition to all those packages – more of those
packages becoming control so you’re making money on the
backend. And with me are Clayton Makepeace. Hi, Clayton.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Hey, how are you doing?
Bob Bly: 	Good, and Wendy.
Julie McManus: 	That’s me the redhead, I’m here.
Bob Bly: 	So, just before we start, a little bit of feedback. We’re back up
with our email system so you can begin sending email questions,
which we will get assuming there’s no more server problems, but
we think we’re back up and running so you can email your
questions as always to feedback@makepeacetotalpackage.com.
Let’s dive into the beginning of Write Better Copy Faster and
produce up to twice as many projects per year.
The first bullet we found from experience our best way to do this is
go down the – what we promised you in the promotion and if you
look at the promotion to which brought you to this call, our first
bullet was five secrets for finishing each project in half the time it
takes you now. So, obviously, a copywriter, royalties aside, a
copywriter has the same problem. If you’re a copywriter and you
work – let’s say you don’t have employees or you don’t farm out
to other copywriters, but you work for, you know, you work for
yourself. You’re a freelance writer.
We have the same problem a dentist has. Dentists have this
expression Clayton, I don’t know if you’ve ever heard this one, if
you’re not drilling and filling, you’re not billing, which means
highly paid as they are, they’re limited by their own labor. If
they’re not in the dental office filling cavities, they don’t make
money and we on the front-end at least, have the same thing.
WARNING:
The following transcript is raw, uncut, and may contain grammatical errors.

-- 1 of 27 --

The Profit Center 	4_Write_Better_Copy_Faster 	Page 2 of 27
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 2 of 27
Obviously, we have income beyond that with royalties and mailing
fees, but on the front-end, you know, if you’re getting paid a flat
fee of $5,000.00 or $10,000.00, or if you write a package that
doesn’t pull a royalty, you just get your upfront money, we have
the same thing.
You know, if you’re getting $1,000.00 a project and you can do
one a week, you make $1,000.00 a week. But if you could do two
weeks, if you could do half the time it takes you now, you can
make – you can do $2,000.00 a week and you suddenly can double
your income for $50,000.00 to $100,000.00 a year. So, I wanna
know how it takes – how you – I have my own methods, but I
wanna know, Clayton, how can people listening to this call do
projects twice as fast or get them done in half the time? What are
your methods?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Well, again, I just want to – I want us to step back from what we’re
doing here for a second and think about copywriting as what it
really is, which is a business. This is – it’s fun, it’s easy, it gives
you freedom, but first and foremost, it’s a business. You’re doing
it for money and how do you measure your income? If someone
asked you how much money you make, you say, “Well, I make
$100,000.00 a year.” There are two components to that, the
amount that you make and how long that you take to make it.
The more projects that you do in a year and can bill for, the more
money you’ll make and so it’s very important to understand that
when projects are moved into next year that money’s lost forever
to this year. This year’s income is reduced by exactly that amount.
So, the number of packages that you can create in a year is
absolutely crucial in order to maximize your income. A lot of
people really don’t think about that and they think that, you know,
all right, so I’ll make the money on January 15 instead of on
December 15.
Well, what you’ve just done is reduced your income by that
amount and that’s really important for clients to know too. A lot of
what I do with – in marketing consultation with my clients is
speeding the process so that we get more mailings per year. You
know, with copywriters everything is bigger winners more often.
With clients, it’s bigger winners, more frequently during the year
so that you get – and bigger mailings more often, and so that’s an
absolute key to this.
In our last call, we talked about – actually, we got a little ahead of
ourselves, we talked a little bit about some of the things that a

-- 2 of 27 --

The Profit Center 	4_Write_Better_Copy_Faster 	Page 3 of 27
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 3 of 27
copywriter can do to finish projects quickly. We talked about
outsourcing research or getting the client to provide the research
for you, we talked about having, you know, compartmentalizing
each project and maybe have a typing service do the long typing
work like me you do work for boardroom and there are a lot of
proof elements that you’re copying directly out of a book or some
other product, a typing service or, you know, I, of course, have a
staff. And I have a girl who is just – absolutely lives on the
keyboard and she types very quickly and saves me days on each
boardroom project by just typing the highlighted copy that I’ve
selected for my proof element.
Another thing to look for is to ask your client when you’re asking
for background research and so forth to ask for digital documents.
I actually have a major, major client that has never converted their
back newsletter issues into PDFs. PDFs are great because in the
little taskbar at the top of the pane, there’s something that says
select and if you push that button, you can scroll through a PDF
and copy any copy out of the PDF and paste it into your Word
document that you want to and that can save you enormous
amounts of time.
Later on we’re gonna be talking about outlines, starter outlines for
packages and how they can save you time. Once you have one of
these outlines that you’re comfortable with and you have it kind of
laid out in a Word document, then it’s very easy to go through and
very quick to go through if you’re doing Word for publishers
particularly, to go through back issues or whatever and copy
directly out of the PDF or another Word document into your
outline and gonna save you hours on every job.
Bob Bly: 	Now, let me issue a warning for the listeners, if you do this, even if
it’s the publisher’s own material, you have to acknowledge the
source. I put in a legend at the top of my copywriter – beginning
of my copywriter say sources, here is where I basically took all the
research, and I’ll have symbols so it’ll be FA equals Federal
Aviation Report.
And the client has a report called the Federal Aviation Report and I
lift half of page 2 and put it in a 20-page promotion, I put in
squiggly brackets the key code FA so they know where I took it
even if it’s their material, they’re gonna ask you. And I’ve actually
been asked – when I didn’t do this, I’ve had clients ask me,
“Where did you get this?” And I’ll say, “It was in the bunch of
stuff you sent me,” and they say, “We need to know where.”

-- 3 of 27 --

The Profit Center 	4_Write_Better_Copy_Faster 	Page 4 of 27
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 4 of 27
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah, I just had that with Agora and actually what I tend to do is I
will – when I copy out of something, I’ll paste it into my outline,
but then I’ll also paste it into a research document that I’m creating
and I tend to go through newsletters for example in order so I’ll
say, on my research document, it’ll say January 2003 and that’s the
issue date and then I’ll copy whatever I want to use out of that,
paste it in the research document, then paste it into my outline.
And then when I’m finished, if anybody wants to come back to me
and say, “Yeah, well, where did you get this number?” You know,
and this actually happened with a major client a week ago, it was
Agora. They were all worried because there were, you know,
dozens of references that they didn’t know where I got them from
and every single one of them was from their own newsletters. And
so all I had to do was find a keyword in my research document and
I was able to tell them what issue and even what page the
information was from.
So, the being able to paste into an outline, will beach the blank
page blues for you and if you’re not feeling creative and have no
idea which direction you’re going to take with the overall, you
know, with the organization of the copy. The outline will save you
time. Also by just pasting the stuff in, it does two things, one,
gives you content immediately you can edit. Secondly, you’ll be
speaking in the editor’s own voice so it’ll pick up the richness of
his own writing style and take on more of his tone.
Bob Bly: 	And that’s terrific.
Clayton Makepeace: 	And then we talked about the, you know, the typing and then also
the final one really was office chores. I tell you what; Wendy just
went to work for me full-time for the first time in our 12 years
together. She has come to work full-time and she’s taken over the
scheduling, bookkeeping, the invoicing, phone chores, and
everything that doesn’t have to do with actually writing copy for
me. And immediately, this is now a 50-hour job – 50-hour a week
job for Wendy and I have to wonder how in the world I ever did it
all.
The thing is when you’re not writing copy, you’re not making
money and when you’re not making money, you’re spending it
because you have ongoing – even if you’re not taking your wallet
out, you have ongoing overhead, ongoing expenses. So, you’re
always in one or two modes, you’re either making it or you’re
spending it. And when you’re answering the phone or doing
scheduling or calling clients that you’re gonna be working for in a

-- 4 of 27 --

The Profit Center 	4_Write_Better_Copy_Faster 	Page 5 of 27
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 5 of 27
couple of months and, you know, juggling schedules or keeping
the books or paying the taxes or doing the invoicing, or any of
those other extraneous jobs, anything that isn’t actual copywriting,
you’re spending money.
And many of us have spouses or significant others that can help
with those tasks that would be happy to do it. As you move along
and you start establishing a decent income from copywriting, you
need to be looking at opportunity costs all the time. Sure, the
bookkeeper to handle, you know, the bookkeeping and invoicing
may cost you a few hundred dollars a month if you hire one, you
know, as – not hire one, but engage your professional service.
It may cost you a few hundred dollars a month, but if you’re a
copywriter and you have the capacity to earn a half million dollars
a year like Carline does or many of us do, then you have to figure
out that every hour that you’re spending doing that, you’re losing a
substantial amount of money. I just – let me digress for a second,
one of the key things that I did early on was that I sat down and I
figured out how much I wanted to make per year and then how
much that was per hour.
That immediately gave me a measuring stick that I could use to
decide which tasks were important enough for me to do myself and
which ones were cheaper to pay someone else to do. So, you just –
you figure 40 hours a week times 52 weeks and you divide the
amount of money that you wanna make per week, per hour, by
that, you know or the amount of money you wanna make a year,
you divide it. And it tells you, oh, well, I’m worth a thousand
dollars an hour that’s my goal. So, if you can find somebody to do
one of these tasks for you for 20 bucks, it’s a no-brainer.
Bob Bly: 	And I’m gonna stop on this for a minute, I tell everyone and I’m
giving a talk to a local New Jersey group next week called NJ
Creatives with their writers and graphic designers and, you know,
to talk on how they can double or triple their income. And the first
thing I tell them is they have to establish a dollar value per hour for
their time and if you meet people, almost no one that you meet
actually does this and, therefore, if you don’t have a dollar value
for your time, the value of that time in your mind is zero. You’ll
always do other things.
I’ll give you an example, I was in the grocery store the other day
on the way home picking something up and I was picking up some
butter and this older gentleman next to me says, “You should put
that back,” and I said, “What?” He says, “Tomorrow, it’ll be a

-- 5 of 27 --

The Profit Center 	4_Write_Better_Copy_Faster 	Page 6 of 27
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 6 of 27
dollar cheaper,” and I smiled at him and I picked the butter up and
put it in my cart and he looked at me like I was nuts. He doesn’t
get the value of time. He thinks I’m losing a dollar and people do
that all the time.
Clayton Makepeace: 	They sure do.
Bob Bly: 	So, if we look at how you can be more productive, the first tip you
gave us is outsource the research, you do this, I do this. When I
start a project, I make a list of the research questions and if the
client hasn’t provided it, I give it to my freelance Internet
researcher. Step No. 2, you say have someone else do the –
guarantee the response device and other copy.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah, I’d like to – I kind of skipped over that one, but I’ve just
hired, you know, a guy who does editorial work. He has no sales
background at all and yet, you know, he’s perfectly equipped to go
through task mail packages the client has done and put together –
and just copy out the guaranteed response device or institutional
copy, biographies, you know, the company history, those kinds of
things.
Now, for me, I was able, you know, I have a full-time guy who’s
helping me with editorial. He’s name is Stephen Banks by the way
and you all are gonna be meeting him in the Total Package in the
weeks ahead. He’s really excellent, and he’s also getting quite an
education. But, you know; even if it’s a typing service, you know,
do those things. I’m sorry, go ahead, Bob.
Bob Bly: 	So, you have someone else do the stuff that is a waste of your time
to do, you know, that someone else who makes much less money
than you make or somewhat less money than you make can do just
as well and just as easily. Step 3, you’ve talked about asking for
digital documents. You know, it used to be where 	client
would send you a big FedEx. Now you say can you email me the
PDFs, can you email me the premiums as a Word document and
you can take – can you email me the issues or get me Web site
access to the issues?
And a lot of the times you can paste – cut and paste not only text,
but images if you wanna use a graph for example. Step 4, you
said, you know, when typing’s needed, have someone else do it. I
talked about it last time, we like optical scanning. You do have to
proof it carefully, but it saves a lot of time so my assistant, my
secretary, has an optical scanner with the OCR software and we
scan stuff where we don’t have an electronic file.

-- 6 of 27 --

The Profit Center 	4_Write_Better_Copy_Faster 	Page 7 of 27
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 7 of 27
And No. 5, you said, you know, you hired Wendy and other people
to do – to run the office and you mentioned things like
bookkeeping, phoning, scheduling – making your schedule and so
forth and I’ve also done that for years and the way I express that to
people, I say, “I have not been to the post office in 17 years.” Why
should you go to the post office or the copy shop when you can
hire a high school kid to do that or college kid to do that for $10.00
an hour?
Clayton Makepeace: 	You know I think as a copywriter, you – goofing off is actually a
better use of your time than mowing the lawn unless you enjoy
mowing the lawn and you find it a relaxing exercise and you
actually do some thinking while you’re doing it. But I really find
that, you know, doing the chores around the house and those other
things that take my time and that I don’t enjoy, those kinds of
chores are not really constructive, but time off can be very
constructive.
I did an article in the Total Package not too long ago, the Human
Brain and Owner’s Manual and in that, we talk about how your
brain works and how when your conscious mind is not fully
engaged, it allows – with a particular subject, you know like for
example, you’re writing a package. Any time that you’re awake or
asleep, you’re away from that work, your mind is still chewing on
it. You may not be thinking about it, but still chewing on it. And,
you know, riding my Harley, going down to the shooting range,
just doing things where I’m goofing off and freeing my mind for
an hour or two tends to really get my juices flowing again and I get
a completely different look at it when I get back.
So, if there are chores that you enjoy doing and that you find kind
of mindless and you don’t mind doing the chore itself, then fine.
But if you’re really trying to maximize your time, I think it’s
important to compartmentalize what you do for a living and assign
tasks to people who can do things cheaper or better in many cases
than you can.
Bob Bly: 	So, it’s okay if you wanna stuff envelopes to stuff them, but make
that a deliberate choice that you’re doing because you want to and
you find that therapeutic. Don’t do it because you have to and do
that instead of doing something where you could get paid for it.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Right.

-- 7 of 27 --

The Profit Center 	4_Write_Better_Copy_Faster 	Page 8 of 27
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 8 of 27
Bob Bly: 	Okay. So, one way to make downtime productive is to do, you
know, is to do – if you’re gonna do any kind of non-creative tasks,
do it as sort of a way to let your mind percolate the ideas you’re
working on. You know, for me it happens to be – I have a
gardener and a lawn service, but raking leaves in the fall serves
that purpose for me. For you it’s shooting and riding a motorcycle.
What else do you do to make downtime productive?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Well, for me, this works great, you know, when I learned – when I
heard that this whole theory about aptitudes, it really helped me a
lot because understanding that you have both the accounting
aptitude and the creative aptitude. And when you’re feeling like
oh, my God, if I have to proofread another word, I’m just gonna
die, you know. What’s happening there is that your accounting
aptitude, your detail-oriented aptitude, your left brain, is exhausted.
It’s had all it can take.
You know the brain’s like any muscle in the body, you know, it
needs exercise, but at some point, it hits the wall. Well, that’s
what happens with both of these aptitudes. So, you know – and the
same thing happens with the creative, you know. You’re sitting
there, you’re working on headlines, you’re doing all kinds of
creative tasks associated with copy, you hit the wall, you know.
And all of a sudden, you know, you just feel like your brain is
mush. You’d be amazed if at those times if you turn to an
accounting-oriented task for detail-oriented left-brain task, just
even for a few minutes that you can come back then to the creative
task later on and you’ll be rejuvenated, you’ll have more energy.
And doing that throughout the day, allows me to put in when I
want to 17-hour days, 18-hour days, go to bed, get six hours of
sleep, get up, do it all over again and especially in starting, you
know, the Total Package and the holding company for that is the
profit center. You don’t hear much about that, but that’s the
company that owns the Total Package. In starting this company
over the last year, it’s required that I do twice the work that I
normally would do. I have my normal clients and then I’m also
building this up to help business owners, marketing execs, and
copywriters.
So, it’s required me to do work two days everyday and having that
approach being able to shift back and forth between the right and
left-brain tasks allows me to put in those kinds of hours even at the
ripe old age of 53 without losing steam during the day. I talk to
copywriters all the time, Arthur Johnson who’s one of the very top
writers in the country right now, is interviewed in our next issue

-- 8 of 27 --

The Profit Center 	4_Write_Better_Copy_Faster 	Page 9 of 27
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 9 of 27
and you, of course, seen the other interviews we’ve done and
there’s a recurring theme that most of these guys say they can only
write for five hours a day and that’s, you know, I can’t write for
more than five hours a day. If I sat down and I just said, well, you
know, that’s what I’m gonna do for the next, you know, for as long
as I can, I’d play out probably around five hours.
But if you do three or four hours of creative writing and three or
four hours of research or sorting tasks or outlining tasks or other
things that are more – less creative more left brain, then you can
extend the amount of time you can stay at work and therefore get
more done. Another way to do it we talked about in the Total
Package as well, but that’s – and I would strongly suggest that
anybody who hasn’t read Psycho Cybernetics, go to Amazon.com
and get it. It’s a wonderful book, it’s been around forever, it’s
updated all the time, but Psycho Cybernetics is basically the study
of how the brain works.
And it was written by a guy who happens to be a plastic surgeon,
who found out that when he went into – as part of his charity work
went into prisons and performed cosmetic surgery on prisoners that
were deformed from birth in some way. They had hair lips or
whatever, cleft palate; he gave them a better look that the
recidivism rate among those inmates, who were now comfortable
with the fact that they showed to the world was a tiny fracture of
what it was for the rest of the prison population. And that
observation made this guy a passionate observer of the functioning
of the brain and how it works.
And in that book, he talks about how your conscious mind is
basically the processing center and the subconscious mind is the
storage center and every experience you have comes in through
the conscious mind and is consciously experienced, but it’s also
recorded in the subconscious and at night when you’re asleep,
every stimulus, every though, everything that happens to you
during the day, everything is sorted by the subconscious mind and
connections are made between that event or that stimulus and
something else that’s happened in your life and that’s what dreams
are.
Dreams are where the subconscious is busy doing that work and
making connections between the things that happen that day, the
feelings that you had, everything with things that had happened in
the past and sometimes that work is observed by the conscious
mind in your sleep just enough of it gets through that it creates a
dream and some of them can be quite vivid and a lot of them make

-- 9 of 27 --

The Profit Center 	4_Write_Better_Copy_Faster 	Page 10 of 27
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 10 of 27
no sense at all because your subconscious is just trying all of these
connections.
But anyway, so the very act of sleeping – I say all the time when
you’re a copywriter, you’re a copywriter 24 hours a day. Well,
you are. You know, if you’re mowing the lawn, you’re going to be
chewing on it. If you’re taking a shower in the morning, either
consciously or subconsciously you’ll be chewing on it and when
you’re asleep at night, your subconscious is making those
connections for you. And the famous story of Tom Edison, who
would be working on a problem he couldn’t solve so he’d take a
nap in his office and when he woke up the answer, would be there
for him.
So, downtime, sleeping is definitely downtime, if you’re talking
about the eight hours you get a night, but also napping. If it
doesn’t interrupt with, you know, getting your good solid hours,
eight hours a night. There’s also another way to accelerate the
writing process. When you come back to the actual work, you’re
going to be – you’re going to see it with new eyes because you’ll
have all those new connections that had been created for you by
your subconscious.
Another way to do it is I make a point that when I’m goofing off,
maybe I’m antique shopping with Wendy or maybe we’re out on
our motorcycles and sitting at a biker bar or something and talking
to people, wherever I am, whatever I’m doing I constantly make a
point and it takes practice at first, but if you just, you know, try to
do it, it becomes habit fairly quickly, is to every hour or so or
several times of the day, just to get back in touch with your
creative muse.
Just kind of – yeah, you’re out, you’re not working, but just think
about the project, think about an unsolved problem, think about a
headline challenge, think about, you know, what is it about that
project that’s bothering me, you know, where – you’ll have a
feeling when you’re writing that – I call it having a stone in my
shoe. There’s just something not right, you know, the copy’s
great, the product’s great, the benefits are there, but there’s just
something not right. And if you sit there and focus on it and try to
figure out what’s right, nine times out of ten you’d just get
frustrated.
But once you get away from the work and you allow your mind not
to obsess on that for a while and then just come back to the work
mentally and think about it from time to time, you’d be surprised

-- 10 of 27 --

The Profit Center 	4_Write_Better_Copy_Faster 	Page 11 of 27
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 11 of 27
how much will come to you that way and that’s why I think for
me, I’ve had some of my best ideas in the shower. I’m not
thinking about work yet, I’m just barely awake, but I’ve had time
away from the work and I’m looking forward to the day ahead and
I think – all of a sudden I think, “Oh, yeah, I gotta solve that
problem. What is wrong with that?” And bang, you know, there’s
the answer –
Bob Bly: 	And do you rush upstairs to the computer to write it down, how do
you make sure you don’t forget that?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Well, I tend not to forget those kinds of things, but I have no
advice for what to do in the shower to remember something, but
make a mental note when you get out, make a note.
Bob Bly: 	Yeah, my wife criticizes me the one thing – habit I have – because
I agree with you, you’re a copywriter 24 hours a day, but she
doesn’t get this and she criticizes that I always walk around, I
don’t know if you noticed it when you sit with me, I have a little
CVS notepad and a pen always in my front-shirt pocket. In fact, I
don’t wear T-shirts because I always wanna wear a shirt where I
can have a pocket for a pad and I am never without a pad.
Clayton Makepeace: 	I think that’s smart. Other people buy these little micro-voice
recorders –
Bob Bly: 	Yeah, the micro-digital voice recorders.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah, they’re smaller than a fountain pen and, you know, you can
– heck, my BMW had a memo function where you just push a
button and you can dictate while you’re driving. I mean, that was
nice. So, and then the other thing is – and it really kind of ties
back in the Tom Edison thing, keep a tablet by your bedside too.
Carry one around with you like Bob does if that helps you or some
kind of a recorder, find a way to keep notes.
But some of the best impressions you’ll get are in those first
waking hours or minutes in the morning. If you’re Wendy, it’s the
few waking hours in the morning. She’s a little slower in the
morning. I’m better in the morning, she’s better at night. But that
time is really important because that’s where you start getting the
feedback from all the work your subconscious did at night.
Bob Bly: 	Makes sense. Any other things that we can think of for – I mean,
these are good suggestions for getting the creativity flowing and
using the downtime or have we covered that?

-- 11 of 27 --

The Profit Center 	4_Write_Better_Copy_Faster 	Page 12 of 27
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 12 of 27
Clayton Makepeace: 	I think that’s it, use both aptitudes, when one gives out, use the
other, use the sleep, the trick – the Edison sleep trick, stay in touch
with your muse even when you’re taking time off, you know, get
your mind back at the project and then keep a way to keep a record
of impressions or thoughts that come to you when you’re not
engaged with the work –
Bob Bly: 	A notepad in the pocket or digital recorder.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yep, absolutely.
Bob Bly: 	Now, the next item we promised is we talked about the six types of
writer’s block and how to overcome each. I had one fairly recently
and you’ve called this in your writings, intimidation. In my case, it
was a package for a product area that I haven’t written about. It
was financial, but it wasn’t stocks, which I’ve done a lot of. It was
another type of investment that I hadn’t done and I just kind of put
it off because I was intimidated. It was just, you know, it just
seemed too difficult. So, what are the six types of writer’s block
that you see the writers you work with encounter and can we get
past them?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Oh, absolutely, you can get past them. I think the first key though
is to recognize what is behind the writer’s block and the first one
you just mentioned for example, intimidation. There are a lot of
different kinds of intimidation. I’m working with young writers,
I’m working with Andrew over in the Netherlands, I’m working
with Daniel up in Canada, several others. And one of the
downsides of this whole doing the total package for me is that it
kind of creates an aura. People see you on the net, they see your
picture, they read your stuff and then when they start writing for
you or working with you, they feel pretty intimidated.
It’s like, you know, in the old days, the copy cuts I had would just
say, oh, yeah, Clayton’s a writer, he’s got a lot of winners, you
know, and I’ve heard of him and I’d love to have him mentor me.
Now, though there’s this whole kind of – I’m sorry, anybody
opposed to R-rated profanity, stop your ears right now, but it’s
bullshit, you know, it’s just bullshit. All of this celebrity stuff is
just –
Bob Bly: 	But it’s a real emotion. Can I tell you something –
Clayton Makepeace: 	It’s a real emotion, but the sooner you can cut through it and get
rid of it and realize it’s not about me, it’s not about you, it’s about

-- 12 of 27 --

The Profit Center 	4_Write_Better_Copy_Faster 	Page 13 of 27
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 13 of 27
the work, then the sooner you can get rid of that kind of
intimidation and be successful. You know, if you’re thinking
about pleasing the senior copywriter who’s mentoring you or if
you’re thinking about anything other than the product and the
customer, then it’s sapping your energies and it’s
counterproductive and so that kind of intimidation is a real
problem. You were gonna say something, Bob?
Bob Bly: 	Yeah, well, give me an example of any time you or a writer you
work with were intimidated when starting a direct mail project and
how you handled it, although I can’t imagine you ever being
intimidated.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Well, okay, well, Andrew, I’m not gonna actually read the email I
sent you, but just two weeks ago, a young writer that shows great
promise and who I knew could do the work or wouldn’t have
gotten the assignment in the first place, began work for me. Now,
I sent him an email and said you’re hired, you know, we worked
out a financial arrangement, and a deadline. He then spent the next
three days telling everyone he knew that he was working with
Clayton Makepeace. He then, you know, went through emotional
swings from excitement to oh, my God, you know, how – what’s
gonna happen when Clayton sees my first draft?
Right now he’s probably smiling and nodding his head. He went
through this whole emotional rollercoaster on this is great, I’m
gonna be a millionaire to oh, my God, well, you know, maybe not
because Clayton’s not paying me a million dollars on my first
project. So, a little disappointment that he’s gonna have to work
his way up. Probably a lot of trepidation over showing his first
draft to me and having me eviscerate it, worried about me kind of
stomping his dream for him by critiquing his copy.
So, he was dealing with all this emotional baggage instead of just
saying, “Look, what is the product, what does it do for the
customer, who is the customer, how does he feel about those
benefits,” and let me focus on that. So, all of that other stuff was
just distracting him so I sent him an email after I saw his first
rough and I just said, “Andrew, get over yourself. This isn’t about
you, it’s not about me, you know, I promise I’ll be gentle with my
crits, you don’t have to worry about it. I don’t expect final copy on
the first rough draft. Just relax, take a deep breath and do the
work.”
So, you know, when you find yourself blocked sometimes,
whenever you find yourself blocked, ask why am I blocked? If the

-- 13 of 27 --

The Profit Center 	4_Write_Better_Copy_Faster 	Page 14 of 27
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 14 of 27
answer’s intimidation, then say, “Oh, I’m intimidated, okay, cool.”
Well, you know, that’s an invalid emotion. You may be feeling it,
but it’s not logical and it’s counter-productive because the outcome
if you produce kick-butt copy, the outcome is going to be
everything that you’re dreaming about now in terms of income,
freedom, all of the other things, the praise from your clients, all of
those things. So, recognize what it is and then deal with whether
or not it’s valid. It’s never valid to be intimidated. I don’t care
who you’re talking to. You know, they put their pants on one leg
at a time. It’s really just about the work.
Another kind of intimidation happened to me years ago when Jay
Abraham started his first newsletter for Phillips and Jay asked for
me to write his launch package for him and I said I would. And
Jay and I had a long history together, we’ve known each other for
years, we never worked together before and so I said, “Sure, I’ll
write the promotion for you.” And the next thing I know, a UPS
truck pulls up and unloaded I think it was 32 	box full of
everything this guy had ever written in his entire career.
Bob Bly: 	And if know Jay, this is a very believable story.
Clayton Makepeace: 	I’ve talked to several other writers who worked with him since
who say that he did the same thing to them. And so, you know,
clients tend not to really enforce deadlines on me a lot, they’d
rather have the quality than, you know, the split-second deadline
thing. And so I always knew every morning when I got up, I knew
that, you know, the client’s not gonna know if I just don’t start on
this today. I mean, I was looking at a garage full of stuff just
sorting through it and trying to figure out what was pertinent and
what wasn’t, was a major challenge and one I wasn’t looking
forward to.
And so day after day I found an excuse to put it off one more day.
I have one of my copy cuts, who is quite successful, has the same
problem. When a new project arrives, whether it’s a 6 or 12-inch
stack of research in a start kit, or a box of stuff, he does the same
thing. He says, “Oh, I just can’t deal with this today. It’s too big a
project, the journey of a thousand miles begins with a single step
thing.” He’s looking at the journey of a thousand miles and
thinking, oh, my God, there’s just too much work to be done here.
He should be looking at, well, all I have to do is cut the tape on the
box and then all I have to do, okay, I’ve done that, now all I have
to do is take that first piece of paper and look at it. Now, I can do
that and if I get that done by noon today, I’ll be a happy guy. And

-- 14 of 27 --

The Profit Center 	4_Write_Better_Copy_Faster 	Page 15 of 27
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 15 of 27
now we’re getting kind of into the mental tricks that I use on
myself. I do that all the time and we’ll talk about that more in a
minute, but this whole idea of, you know, I don’t have to write a
package today. All I have to do is open the box and sort through
the paper –
Bob Bly: 	And maybe put them into files or something like that.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah, and then maybe do a little scanning and then tonight when I
go to bed, my subconscious will sort things out a little bit. I’ll be
more on top of it tomorrow and then tomorrow what I’m gonna do
is I’m gonna start going through and highlighting things that I
think I can use. You know, this is a – on longer copy especially,
this is a week’s long process of actually producing something you
wanna show the client. So, you know, understand that if you’re
feeling blocked and you just can’t get into it, it could be that kind
of intimidation just being intimidated by the amount of work that
lays ahead.
Bob Bly: 	And your solution is what the psychologist call for educating
children, I don’t know if you’ve heard this, called chunking. If a
kid says I can’t do the homework, you say, “Well, you know, why
don’t you just do these five problems, and then okay, when you’re
done with that, you do this page 3, and then we’re done with that,
just do this worksheet tonight.” You chunk it and that makes it so
much easier. I have a child who has ADD and that’s what we do
with him and, you know, if it could help you, it could help anyone
get through, you know, this think of being overwhelmed.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Absolutely.
Bob Bly: 	It’s a great technique. So, because No. 1 is intimidation of writer’s
block and you can chunk things and then the other thing is you
said, you know, if you’re intimidated, focus on the work, not who
it is. I mean, I’ve certainly felt intimidated. You mentioned
people intimidated working with you. On the few times I’ve
worked with celebrities or people I thought were really much
smarter than I am, I also have been intimidated and that was great
advice, which I didn’t have. It’s forget about them, forget about
you just do the work.
Clayton Makepeace: 	You know you say you have a child who’s ADD and we have a
son who had some issues early on too and part of the chunking
process is kind of the – it’s very similar to what we were talking
about compartmentalizing. You know, you compartmentalize the
different parts of the task in order to identify which ones you could

-- 15 of 27 --

The Profit Center 	4_Write_Better_Copy_Faster 	Page 16 of 27
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 16 of 27
do better than anyone and which ones will reward you and which
ones would be better to delegate. But there’s another part of that
too and it’s thinking as a copywriter, thinking through the process
of writing your sales’ message. What is the most effective way to
begin, what’s the most effective second step, what’s the third step
and so forth.
Once you’ve said, “Okay, well, the first thing that I have to do is I
have to read everything I can about this guy’s product.” You go,
“Okay, fine.” You know, I can make a nice reading day, I can grab
a briefcase and chuck a bunch of this stuff in there, go down to
Barnes and Noble, grab myself a latte and sit there and read for a
few hours. That’s fine, that’s great, that’s not intimidating, it’s
fun.
But in order to chunk, you first have to have sequencing, you have
to know what’s first, what’s second, what’s third, what’s fourth
and then within each one of those steps, you can even slice and
dice it more finely if you need to if those tasks take longer periods
of time. So, sequencing, chunking, that kind of stuff really helps
with copywriters too because frankly, we’re all ADD.
Julie McManus: 	Can I interject something there just because this reminds me a lot
of Carlene’s interview where she talked a lot about the blank-page
syndrome and she had a lot of great advice about how to break out
the copy in different sections, and, you know, just fill in the reply
page. Now, you’re one page less that you have to fill in and that
was I thought very helpful. So, people could refer back to that
issue, it was only like two issues ago I believe.
Clayton Makepeace: 	I think that’s smart and don’t assume that the best way to proceed
is to begin at the beginning. I know people who get blocked by
writing a headline for days, you know. I think that’s nuts –
Bob Bly: 	Yeah, you said just forget the headline, put in a placeholder.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Forget it, forget it. If the best way for you to get a grasp on the
product is to write the offer and response first, do it. You know, at
least once you’ve done that, you know what you’re selling. If the
best way for you to begin is to write the copy about the free gift
that the person gets or the series of free gifts, do that first, you
know –
Bob Bly: 	So, you’re writing a section where you’re making a substantial
argument about an investment and you’re at the point where you

-- 16 of 27 --

The Profit Center 	4_Write_Better_Copy_Faster 	Page 17 of 27
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 17 of 27
don’t understand it and you know what to go onto, but you can’t
get past it. Write blah, blah, blah and go on.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Um-hmm.
Bob Bly: 	And you –
Clayton Makepeace: 	I do that all the time. Yadda, yadda, yadda, right?
Bob Bly: 	Yeah, exactly. So, we know how to give them – one cause of
writer’s block is that we get intimidated and we shouldn’t. A next
cause you had told me is procrastination.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah, I’m in favor of procrastination –
Bob Bly: 	Yeah, you are. I’m against it. I think it’s – but tell me why.
Clayton Makepeace: 	It’s one of my favorite things to do –
Bob Bly: 	I like it too, but you’re gonna pay for it.
Clayton Makepeace: 	No, you’re not.
Bob Bly: 	You don’t think so?
Clayton Makepeace: 	No, not if you do it right. The key to procrastination is picking the
times to procrastinate. We all love doing procrastination because
what happens is, you know, you’re thinking about this big thing
that you have to do and then when you give yourself permission to
put it off, you feel so free and so wonderful and like they’ve just
lifted a whole burden off your shoulders and like the whole day is
a blank slate in front of you and that you can do anything you
wanna do with that. It’s too addictive and too wonderful a feeling
to – it’s like weight loss, there’s no amount of willpower that’s
gonna make you lose weight.
You know, there’s no amount of willpower that’s gonna make you
stop procrastinating, everyone’s gonna do it. The key is to do it at
intelligent times. What I do – call it creative procrastination, okay.
If you’re judicious about it, I was talking to a writer who was
having these problems getting started, called it writer’s block.
What it was, was there were some other things going on in his life
that were pretty exciting and pretty fun and he just couldn’t bring
himself to get away from those things and focus on the work at
hand.

-- 17 of 27 --

The Profit Center 	4_Write_Better_Copy_Faster 	Page 18 of 27
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 18 of 27
And the problem was with this guy that there was always
something going on in his life that he would rather do, okay. And
so he was always weeks late with every job and so what I told him
was just pick your time. Say I will never procrastinate until I have
read the assignment, okay. After I’ve read the assignment, I’m
going to allow myself to put – to take a day off, you know, or to
reward myself in some other way. So, you choose the times to
procrastinate –
Bob Bly: 	So, choose your times.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Now what’s happening is you’ve read the research so now you go
and you take your day off. What have we just talked about?
You’re a writer 24/7, right. So, you’re taking your day off and
you’re doing things, but you’ve loaded your brain up with
everything about this project and you’ve done some thinking, your
subconscious has done its sorting now you’re doing your
procrastination. You’re not really procrastinating, you’re working
on the job even though you’re doing something fun.
So, I love procrastination. I love to do it, I don’t do it enough and
– but the key is timing. Just, you know, pick your times so you can
get enough loaded up into your brain so that when you’re doing
other things and enjoying a beautiful fall day like it is here now,
your brain is still chewing on the project.
Bob Bly: 	Okay, so overcoming writer’s block, getting past intimidation
No. 1, creative procrastination No. 2. Well, let’s talk about No. 3,
which I think is related. You said it is a beautiful fall day so don’t
you get distracted and, you know, what happens when you just say,
you look out the window and say, “I don’t wanna do this.” Should
you just hang it up for the day or should you keep going?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Well, sometimes, you know, the least productive time that you’re
going to have is when you decide, you know, I just have to gut this
out. That’s when you’re gonna be getting the blank-page
syndrome big time. You know, you say, “Ah, I just have to gut
this out. I just have to spend this time.”
Bob Bly: 	That’s the worse.
Clayton Makepeace: 	That’s the worst because your output is gonna stink, you’re not
gonna be creative. You k now, there are times when you just do
have to hang it up, Bob. I mean, I do. As you move on through
your life and you do this for longer and longer periods of time
though, I think you’ll find that, you know, that happens less and

-- 18 of 27 --

The Profit Center 	4_Write_Better_Copy_Faster 	Page 19 of 27
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 19 of 27
less often. It certainly does for me. But every once and a while, I
have to give myself permission to just allow the distraction to take
over. If you’re sitting there and you’re working and there’s a rock
band practicing next door, you know, what’s the point of even
sitting there, right.
So, you don’t need that kind of frustration so the smart thing to do
is to – well, with me, I feel guilty if I’m not working. It’s just me I
guess –
Bob Bly: 	Me too.
Clayton Makepeace: 	I’ve been in business for myself 33 years –
Bob Bly: 	We have what’s called the puritan work ethic.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah, I guess so. And, you know, every day I know what I do
provides for my family very directly. There’s no illusion that it’s
coming through paycheck or through, you know, it’ll always be
there whether I produce or not. I know the quality and quantity
work I do is going to directly, you know, there’s a direct link
between the quality and quantity of the work I do and the amount
of money that I’ll have to lavish on my family.
And so if I take time off for any reason for any selfish reason, I
feel guilty and it’s taken me an awful long time to just learn to give
myself permission to do that, to understand that that kind of thing,
you know, if you’re distracted, it’s not your fault, you’ve done
everything you can to make it work. It’s just not working today.
Then, yeah, just go for it.
Bob Bly: 	Well, I’m like you, I feel guilty if I’m not working. I had one that
happened yesterday. I’d given a speech in Washington at the
DMA in the morning and I was done real early. It was an 8:30
speech, done at 10:30, got right in the car, came back here and
said, “Oh, I’ll have plenty of time to work.” I don’t know why, but
the five-hour drive just kind of tired me, maybe because I ate
Popeye’s Chicken on the way back and that’s no good for me.
But I was so tired and I sat here for two hours and I didn’t do
anything. What do you do when you’re not working because
you’re just – you’re sick or you’re tired or you didn’t sleep well,
what do you do then, you can’t get pass that, you just give it up?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Well, I think when it comes to energy, energy is creativity,
creativity is energy and I think it’s important to be preemptive

-- 19 of 27 --

The Profit Center 	4_Write_Better_Copy_Faster 	Page 20 of 27
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 20 of 27
when it comes to energy. That Popeye’s Chicken cost you some
money.
Bob Bly: 	Yeah, but it was worth it.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Well, everything in life is a transaction –
Bob Bly: 	Yes, it is, it’s a trade off.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah, and you made a transaction and what you make a couple
thousand dollars an hour and you lost four hours work so that
chicken cost you eight grand.
Bob Bly: 	Yeah, but it was the spicy so it was worth it.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah.
Bob Bly: 	And I had the rice and beans.
Clayton Makepeace: 	And if that’s the case, then cool, you know, good for you. You
made a good – you’re a good consumer, you spent your money
wisely and you’re a satisfied customer.
Bob Bly: 	Of course now you’re making me feel stupid about it, but we get
your point. That’s good what you said though, energy is creativity.
If you aren’t tired or sick, you can’t be – I don’t think you can be
creative without high energy.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Right. And I think the only time that I – what I try to do and again,
I hate rules and I tend not to be real good following them, but what
I try very hard to do is to understand that energy is – the amount of
energy you’re gonna have and therefore, the amount of creativity
you’re going to have is directly related to other choices that you
make in your life and diet is one of them. And, you know, if
you’re eating junk, if you’re eating a lot of fried foods, if you’re
not taking supplements, if you’re not getting the nutrition you need
–
Bob Bly: 	And exercise.
Clayton Makepeace: 	If you’re not getting exercise, if you’re going out in the evenings
and getting hammered and coming to work with a hangover, you
know, you’re basically sabotaging yourself. You’re putting
yourself in a position where when you’re at your desk the next day,
you’re not gonna be creative because you have no energy. And so,
you have to understand the 24/7 thing again here, you know.

-- 20 of 27 --

The Profit Center 	4_Write_Better_Copy_Faster 	Page 21 of 27
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 21 of 27
When you’re working out, you’re being a copywriter. The end
result of that workout is gonna be greater energy, right, and that
means greater creativity.
When you’re making good food choices the same is true. Right,
when you’re taking your supplements and making sure that you’re
well nourished, you’re being a good copywriter and when you’re
deciding if you’re going to drink, to drink only on the weekends,
you’re being a good copywriter or only drinking before you’re
gonna have to work. I mean, when I was younger, I could drink
every night and I was just fine in the morning, right. Now I find if
I drink on the weekend, it takes me one or even two days to get
back up to speed.
So, you know, recognize objectively, you know, no excuses. The
choice you make affects the amount of energy you have and so
when you’re making a choice that’s going to sap energy in the days
ahead, recognize that you’re spending your own money, you’re
losing productivity, you’ll produce fewer packages this year, you’ll
make less money this year and so, you know, keep that in mind
when you’re making those choices.
Bob Bly: 	That’s absolutely true. Now, another problem I’ve had that has
stopped me sometimes from getting out as much as I want to,
you’ve used the term to describe this, you call it post-partum
depression for copywriters. What do you mean by that? I know
what you mean, but –
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah, well, that’s – yesterday, I delivered this project that has been
plaguing me for about three months and I’ve had a lot of
interruptions, I couldn’t get my mind around the product and the
interruptions that we’ve had have been major. You know, we’ve
moved into new offices, we’re hiring employees, we’ve got – had
business trips, with all this kind of stuff and I had a lot of other
things going to this client so I didn’t feel particularly pressured to
get it done really quickly. And so I had the luxury of time and I’m
sure there was some, not much, but maybe a little bit of
procrastination in there.
But anyway, yesterday at long last I got the damn thing off my
desk and I emailed it to the client and I felt – and I do every time I
deliver one of these things, I felt like I just had a baby. Wendy
gets mad at me when I say that because obviously, you know –
Bob Bly: 	You and I don’t really know what it’s like to have a baby.

-- 21 of 27 --

The Profit Center 	4_Write_Better_Copy_Faster 	Page 22 of 27
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 22 of 27
Clayton Makepeace: 	She’s executive in charge of having babies at our house and she’s
actually done it a couple of times and doesn’t think that, you know,
what I do in a month or two of my time can compare to what it
took her nine months to produce and I agree with her objectively,
but I still feel like I’ve had a baby. And so, at that moment, you
know the last thing you want to do is to dive right into another
project. And I frankly don’t have a solution for that one other than
take the day off. Carlene talks about this and I think it’s
wonderful. When she delivers a package or when she gets a
royalty check, that’s an automatic day off, absolutely automatic
day off.
Next day there’s no way she’s working and not only is she not
working, she’s gonna go out and spend money. She’s gonna go
buy something for herself and I think, you know, for the obvious
reasons, I think that’s a wonderful idea, you know, it’s motivating,
it’s rewarding. We all need short-term rewards as well as long-
term. In my business where I do a lot of direct mail, I’ll write a
package in February, it’ll be finalized in March, it’ll get through
graphics in April, it’ll be tested in May, it’ll do its first rollout in
July. I won’t see a royalty on that package probably until August,
okay.
So, the work I’m doing in February, the real reward for that work
isn’t until August. Now how you stay motivated about that? You
have to find little reward steps along the way, you know. If I get
this done, then I’m going to allow myself that. And setting those
times up in the progress of a job, I think is absolutely crucial. You
know, when I get the first rough, the first complete rough done, not
even something I’m gonna show my client, I’m gonna do this for
myself, you know.
And it’s important to make sure the rewards aren’t things like –
that are gonna actually slow the process like, you know, I’m gonna
go out and get hammered or I’m gonna take a trip to the Caribbean
or something, you know, that is way out of whack or way out of
perspective, but you have something short-term to look forward to.
So, you need the baby steps as well as the big steps. You’re
looking for the big reward long-term, but in the short-term, reward
yourself.
Well, when you deliver a package, that’s definitely one of those
times and finding something that you love and saying I’m not
gonna do that until I finish this package, but when I do finish it,
I’m giving myself permission to take a day or, you know, a three-

-- 22 of 27 --

The Profit Center 	4_Write_Better_Copy_Faster 	Page 23 of 27
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 23 of 27
day weekend or whatever and do this great thing. So, anyway, I
think that’s a great idea.
Bob Bly: 	No, that is. Let me stop for a second and give our listeners a
timekeeping issue. As always, Clayton has more content than even
I anticipate so I think this a great value, but we’re almost at the
1:00 mark and we’ve gotten through only three or four of the seven
bullets. So, what I’d like to do is this, we’ve negotiated with you
guys listening before that we can go and you don’t seem to mind
an extra ten minutes, you know, without advance warning, which
is what I’d like to do today.
And of course, if you have to ring off, you’ll be able to listen on
the Web audio so you won’t miss anything. What I’d like to do
now and we’ll remind you for next week, I’d like to extend again
as a bonus, Clayton, if this is all right with you, next week’s call
from 60 to 90 minutes.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Before we move on too much, I just wanna say one more thing
here about this whole thing of writer’s block. One of the strange
situations that you’re going to find yourself in as a writer is you sit
down – you go down to your desk in the morning and it’s time to
work and you’re broke and you’ve got a month’s worth of bills that
are due and you have no money. And now you’re supposed to be
creative while you’re worrying the whole time in the back of your
mind about how you’re gonna put food on the table for the next
two or three weeks.
I went through this all the time when I was starting out. How you
focus on the work when the phone’s ringing and it’s a bill
collector, how do you focus on the work when, you know, maybe
the bill collectors aren’t calling, but you know in a week or two,
you’ve got a rent payment or a mortgage payment due and you
don’t have the cash, how do you do that?
Bob Bly: 	Or forget the creditors when you have a parent or spouse or
significant other who didn’t maybe approve of you doing this and
saying, “I don’t see any money coming in. You gonna get a real
job soon and give this up?”
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah, absolutely.
Bob Bly: 	That’s the worst pressure.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah, it is. And frankly, I don’t know how to get over that one,
the spouse thing not being fully on board. I had an email from a

-- 23 of 27 --

The Profit Center 	4_Write_Better_Copy_Faster 	Page 24 of 27
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 24 of 27
couple where the man really wanted to get into this copywriting
thing and was passionate about it and his wife wasn’t supporting
him and she was telling him that everyone that tells you that
copywriting is a good profession, they’re just trying to sell you
something and, you know, it’s just a big scam and a big con game
and so she was extremely negative and at the same time he was
extremely positive. And I just – I said – I told him, you know, “I
don’t wanna talk to you. I wanna talk to the two of you because
frankly if your spouse isn’t on board, this isn’t gonna work.”
And so I replied to the two of them and I just basically told them
that this is real, we are making excellent money as copywriters, not
as people who teach copywriting, but the teaching has not made
me a penny. It’s cost me $150,000.00 in the last 12 months setting
this whole thing up. The money that I’ve made has been
100 percent from royalties from clients and I said so it is real and I
can introduce you to many other people – many other successful
writers who can show you it’s real. But don’t do it if you can’t
support each other in it because it is starting a new business and
it’s always tough at the beginning when you start a new business.
So, that’s the only advice I have there.
But I do have some other advice and I gave it to my son-in-law,
who decided to become a direct response graphic artist. And that
advice is no matter what, pay yourself first. When you get a check
in, sock – set a number and 10 percent is the absolute minimum,
but set a number of that money that some percent that you are
gonna sock away and that you are not gonna touch no matter what.
The only time you get to touch that money is if you come up on a
dry spell and it could be, you know, there are a lot of reasons this
business goes through dry spells, the financial markets may have
gotten very mature or taken a lot of hits and the people aren’t
buying financial products right now and that could go on – I’ve
seen it gone on before for a month or two. More recently it went
on for a year, a little over a year before it finally started picking up
again.
During those dry spells, you’ve got to have reserves, you have to
have money set aside or you’re gonna be sitting there staring at
major financial problems and that’s gonna be – you’ll be obsessing
on those instead of trying to figure out ways of how to solve them
and you can either worry about a financial problem or you can set
about solving it. And the problem is that the worry keeps you from
solving it because you’re gonna be obsessing and thinking
negatively about all of the terrible things that can happen if you
don’t come up with a bunch of money instead of working hard and

-- 24 of 27 --

The Profit Center 	4_Write_Better_Copy_Faster 	Page 25 of 27
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 25 of 27
doing the things that’s gonna be necessary to bring in money to
solve the problem.
So, this is just the real nuts and bolts, nitty-gritty of the business.
There are dry spells, you’ve got to pay yourself first. And, you
know, one of the things – and a lot of people don’t have a lot of
financial discipline and I’m certain infamous for that one. I found
having a safe deposit box or even a storage box at MonX or
someplace like that and buying gold coins or, you know, socking
cash away in it. In other words, make it hard to spend that money.
Don’t keep it in a checking account where you can just flash a
debit card and start drawing it down. Make it a deliberate, difficult
decision to get to that money and only use it if you absolutely have
to, to pay the mortgage while you’re getting cash flow going again.
Bob Bly: 	I agree. Another great way to do it, get enough to put a deposit on
a house and buying investment property. You’re much more likely
to sell it. You’re much more unlikely to just turn around and sell it
and spend it, you’ll keep it.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Absolutely.
Bob Bly: 	So, we’re almost at the end here. What I’d like to do is defer until
next week, which we’re gonna extend – we’re gonna extend next
week’s call, which is the fourth in our series is the secret that
pushed Clayton’s income to well over one million per year, which
is the session, although I like – well, three, this is the session I’m
waiting for because I’m not at the million a year mark and that’s
my goal so I’m hoping he’ll – I know he’ll tell me how to do it and
we’ll cover what’s on the agenda for that. But we’re also gonna
move – we’re gonna add a half hour and first, we’re gonna do two
items from this week, the No. 1 productivity killer and the three
mental tricks that kick its butt every time, which we sort of done,
but we’ll review that next week.
Some of this we’ve already covered a little, but to answer the last
bullet of this week, you promised us six ways to keep your creative
juices flowing even when you think your brain is toast and how do
you – because you can’t always say I’m gonna take a break or a
day off. Sometimes even if you have a client who’s understanding
about deadlines, sometimes the deadlines are coming and you’ve
got to do the work, how do you keep creative maybe when you’re
kind of at the end of your rope.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Well, I think we’ve covered most of these, Bob.

-- 25 of 27 --

The Profit Center 	4_Write_Better_Copy_Faster 	Page 26 of 27
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 26 of 27
Bob Bly: 	Wanna go over them quickly?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Sure. Well, No. 1 keep the momentum going and you can do that
by – as we talked about shifting from right to left brain tasks
during the day. The minute – and it’s like – one of the things I’ve
learned when riding a motorcycle over long distances is if you stop
every hour, your butt will hold up for the entire trip. You can ride
coast to coast. If your first hour on that bike, you do two hours, the
rest of that trip you’re gonna be sore. So, before you reach the
fatigue mark, before you get to the point where you’re hurting,
where you’ve hit the wall, you know, shift off. You know, try to
schedule your work so that you’re shifting right to left-brain.
The second one is the idea of taking a 15-minute accounting break
if you’re working on creative tasks and you’re blocked. Add up a
column of numbers, divide it by pie, you know, do – and do it
manually. Do something that involves, fully involves the left side
of the brain, then return to it and I think in 15 minutes, you can be
back up in running again. But again, you have to do it early not
after you hit the wall and have been struggling creatively for an
hour or two. By then it’s gonna require more than just that
15-minute break, but if you just work that in during the day, it’ll
help.
And we talked about doing detail-oriented personal tasks, balance
your checkbook, pay the taxes, do something like that, that’s
another way to keep your productivity, your creativity going. We
talked about mowing the lawn, doing something enjoy doing that
allows your brain to disengage and just kind of flirt with the work
rather than really, you know, being fully engaged with it. We
talked about the napping approach and we talked about how
sometimes all you can do is hang it up and when you’ve tried
everything else and you just can’t get it and you don’t have detail-
oriented tasks to do, then go ahead and hang it up and forgive
yourself for it. You know, it’s okay. It’s okay not to feel creative,
it’s okay not to feel motivated when you’ve hit the wall.
Everybody has those times, but don’t then go do something self-
destructive. Some of the greatest writers ever like Hemmingway
for example, the writing brought money, money brought freedom,
freedom brought alcohol, alcohol brought writer’s block, you
know, and then finally that ended very badly in a suicide. I mean,
that was a self-destructive cycle where he was using unhealthy
things to compensate for his inability to write or for his block
creativity. So, you know, keep that in mind as you’re choosing

-- 26 of 27 --

The Profit Center 	4_Write_Better_Copy_Faster 	Page 27 of 27
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 27 of 27
what to do in the times when you’re hanging it up and just walking
away.
Bob Bly: 	Makes total sense. And on that note, we will end for today and
next week we’re gonna do – we’re gonna finish the couple of items
from this week and move into next week that’ll be our first half
hour from noon to 12:30 next week and then we’ll go from 12:30
to 1:30 on our scheduled session, conference for the secret that
pushed Clayton Makepeace’s income to well over one million per
year, which is I think something only a few copywriters have ever
done and you probably were the first I would think. I mean,
there’s only a handful.
Clayton Makepeace: 	I think Gary Bencivenga probably had some million-dollar years
too before he retired. But the things we’re gonna be talking about
have really took me very close to three million a year.
Bob Bly: 	Yeah, I don’t think anybody’s doing that so you’re gonna hear how
to make more money at a level very few copywriters, you know,
including me have reached. So, I’m eager and we’ll see you guys
next week. And again, remember, feedback, you know, if you
wanna email anything. I did not get any email questions. I hope
there were none. If they were sent over our server, then perhaps
there was a problem and we will get to them next week as well.
But you can send your questions and comments to
feedback@makepeacetotalpackage.com. All right, folks, thanks
and we’ll speak to you next week.
